askNext: What exact meaning of Fallback policy is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
fallback-policy.body: Fallback policy belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.
fallback-policy.example: "The fallback policy discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
fallback-policy.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
fallback-policy.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

fallback-policy.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.
fallback-policy.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Model Router are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.

askNext: What exact meaning of Fallback routing is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
fallback-routing.body: Fallback routing belongs in the dictionary because implementation detail changes the product conversation. The useful question is not whether the term sounds modern, but what constraint, cost, interface, or failure mode it names.
fallback-routing.example: "The team said fallback routing as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."
fallback-routing.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
fallback-routing.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A concrete implementation concern wearing a compact label.

fallback-routing.warningLabel: Technical vocabulary is not implementation evidence.
fallback-routing.whyExists: It exists because conversations around OpenRouter need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.

askNext: What behaviour are we trying to change that prompting cannot hold steady?
What data will teach that behaviour without teaching unwanted shortcuts?
How will we know the tuned model is better, not just narrower?
fine-tuning.body: Good fine-tuning is usually boring in the best way. It removes repeated prompt hacks and makes behaviour steadier. Bad fine-tuning makes a model narrower, more brittle, and harder to reason about.
fine-tuning.example: "They did not fine-tune because the team loved custom models; they did it because the required output format had to be boringly consistent."
fine-tuning.misuse: It is often sold as the universal answer whenever prompts disappoint. In reality, many problems blamed on a lack of fine-tuning are retrieval problems, product design problems, or evaluation problems. Fine-tuning can help, but it can also overfit and create fresh failure modes.
fine-tuning.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means curating a task-specific dataset, deciding how much of the base model to adapt, training on that smaller dataset, and checking whether the resulting behaviour improved enough to justify the effort and operational complexity.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A targeted attempt to move model behaviour with additional training rather than endless prompt cajoling.

fine-tuning.whyExists: General-purpose models are broad, not bespoke. Fine-tuning exists for cases where prompting alone is too unstable, too expensive, or too weak to reliably shape behaviour.

fluid-compute.example: "Fluid Compute came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
fluid-compute.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
fluid-compute.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

translations.label: procurement meaning
translations.text: Treat the name as a dependency to inspect, not a badge to admire.

fluid-compute.warningLabel: A brand-shaped noun is not a technical specification.
fluid-compute.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Vercel often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.

askNext: Frontier by which metric: benchmark spread, multimodal breadth, tool use, or cost-to-quality ratio?
Does the use case actually need a top-tier model, or just a reliable one?
What trade-offs come with using the most capable model available?
frontier-model.body: The term becomes most useful when followed immediately by the phrase "for what task?" Otherwise it tends to drift into status perfume.
frontier-model.example: "The company pitched a frontier-model strategy. Operations translated that into a calmer sentence about high cost, external dependency, and unclear product fit."
frontier-model.misuse: It is often treated as a formal badge rather than a loose ranking label. Vendors, commentators, and investors may use it to imply inevitability, superiority, or strategic importance beyond what the actual use case warrants.
frontier-model.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a large, expensive model family with broad capability, strong benchmarks, and significant operational or policy attention. For many products, the question is not whether a model is frontier. The question is whether it is necessary.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: This company is close enough to the top of the capability race to sound strategically important.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A high-end model near the current performance frontier, whether or not your workload needs that much firepower.

frontier-model.warningLabel: Useful shorthand, not a formal category. Often as much status signal as technical description.
frontier-model.whyExists: The term exists because the field needed a way to talk about the leading edge of capability without listing every model individually. It can be useful shorthand when discussing high-end systems, their risks, and their costs.

askNext: Who validates the arguments before execution?
What tools is the model allowed to call, and under what conditions?
How are failures, partial successes, and risky actions surfaced?
function-calling.body: Function calling is one of the clearest places where AI product language can mislead. The impressive part is not merely that the model chose a tool. The important part is whether the surrounding system kept that choice legible and safe.
function-calling.example: "The assistant did not refund the customer by itself; it produced a structured request, the service checked policy, and only then did the refund tool run."
function-calling.misuse: It gets described as if the model itself is performing the external action. Usually it is not. The model proposes the call. Your software validates it, executes it, and handles the consequences. If that surrounding code is sloppy, the elegance of the schema will not save you.
function-calling.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means JSON schemas, argument validation, permission controls, retries, and post-tool reasoning. Useful systems treat function calling as structured intent, not an excuse to trust a model near production infrastructure.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model emits a structured request; the real system still has to decide whether to trust, reject, or execute it.

function-calling.whyExists: Models are good at interpreting language but bad at directly mutating real systems without control. Function calling exists to separate decision-making about which action to take from the actual execution of that action.
